Q1: My study routine was honestly a bit chaotic, but I made it work. I usually tried to carve out time in the evenings after my other responsibilities, sitting down with my laptop and going through readings and notes. I'm a read-write learner, so I spent a lot of time typing out summaries and rewriting key concepts. Some weeks I'd manage consistent sessions, but other weeks everything would pile up and I'd have to cram. I aimed for about 15 hours a week, but it wasn't always evenly spread out.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on deadlines and how I was feeling emotionally. When assignments were due, I'd be studying late into the night for several days straight. During calmer weeks, I tried to space things out more evenly, but honestly my attendance being around 50% tells you that sometimes life just got in the way. I dealt with a lot of stress and anxiety, so some days I just couldn't show up even when I wanted to.
Q3: Usually a deadline or an assignment prompt would get me to log in. I'd also log in when I felt like I was falling behind and needed to catch up on videos or discussion posts. Sometimes it was just anxiety pushing me to check if I'd missed something important. The fear of missing a deadline was a big motivator, even on my worst days.
Q4: After a few weeks, logging in did become more automatic for me. I started checking the LMS every couple of days just to see what was new or if there were announcements. Having seven online courses meant I was constantly juggling platforms, so I trained myself to check regularly. It became part of my evening routine alongside making tea and settling in to study.
Q5: The video lectures were incredibly helpful for me—I watched almost all of them and took detailed notes while they played. I also found the discussion boards useful because they helped me see how other students interpreted the material. Reading through different perspectives sometimes clarified concepts I was struggling with. The written resources like PDFs and articles were my go-to since I learn best through reading and writing.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the peer interaction features. I know there were opportunities to collaborate, but I just didn't have the energy for it with everything else going on. My anxiety made it hard to reach out to classmates I didn't know. I also sometimes skipped optional readings when I was overwhelmed, telling myself I'd come back to them later, which didn't always happen.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to assignments or exams. If I knew it would help me understand something I'd be tested on, I was all in. I also valued activities that let me engage at my own pace—things I could pause, reread, or return to. Anything that felt like busywork without clear purpose, I struggled to prioritize.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different. Required ones had this weight of obligation that pushed me through even on bad days. Optional ones felt like luxuries I couldn't afford when I was already stretched thin with stress and coursework. Sometimes I'd look at optional materials and feel guilty for not doing them, but I had to be realistic about my energy levels.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying offline. I'd download PDFs and readings to my laptop and work through them without being connected. I also kept a physical notebook where I'd write out summaries and practice answers. A lot of my actual learning happened with printed notes spread across my desk, completely invisible to any online system.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd watch a video once, then spend hours afterward reviewing my handwritten notes and rewriting key points. The LMS would only show that I watched the video for 90 minutes or so, but the real studying happened after. I also used external websites and YouTube videos to supplement concepts I didn't fully grasp from the course materials alone.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a reading or download a lecture slide once, then work with it offline for days. My internet isn't always reliable, so I learned to download everything I might need. I'd annotate PDFs on my laptop and review them multiple times without ever logging back into the LMS.
Q12: There were definitely periods where engaging became really hard. When my anxiety spiked or when I was feeling particularly sad, I'd withdraw from everything. Sometimes multiple deadlines across my seven courses would collide, and I'd feel paralyzed. Those were the times my attendance dropped and I'd miss classes, even though I was still trying to keep up with materials at home.
Q13: Workload and fatigue affected me significantly. With seven online courses, I was constantly juggling competing demands. When I was exhausted or stressed, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without really processing anything. Confusion about assignment requirements sometimes made me avoid starting tasks until the last minute because I didn't know where to begin.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd scroll through materials or have a video playing but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd be thinking about everything stressing me out instead of actually absorbing the content. I'd finish a reading and realize I couldn't remember a single thing from it. Those moments were frustrating because I was technically 'studying' but getting nothing from it.
Q15: I postponed things more than I'd like to admit, especially when my anxiety was high. Starting an assignment felt overwhelming, so I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt better. Sometimes I avoided discussion posts because the thought of putting my ideas out there for others to see made me nervous. I knew these things were important, but my brain just wouldn't cooperate.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my lifeline and my enemy at the same time. They forced me to get things done when I otherwise might have kept postponing. But they also caused intense stress spikes where I'd be working frantically at the last minute. My LMS activity probably looked very bursty—quiet for days, then suddenly very active right before something was due.
Q17: Feedback motivated me to try harder, especially when it was constructive. Good feedback made me feel like my effort was worth it, which helped counter my low motivation. Critical feedback sometimes stung and made me anxious about future submissions, but I tried to use it to improve. After my CBT sessions, I got better at not catastrophizing negative feedback.
Q18: The course structure helped me stay on track when I was struggling. Having clear modules and deadlines gave me something concrete to work toward. But when the structure was too rigid or didn't account for students dealing with multiple courses, it felt suffocating. I appreciated courses that had some flexibility built in for people like me who were managing a lot.
Q19: Clear instructions and well-organized modules made engagement easier. When I could see exactly what was expected and when, I felt less anxious. What made it harder was when resources were scattered or when there was too much content without guidance on what to prioritize. Discussion requirements also felt harder for me because of my anxiety around peer interactions.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is genuinely trying but struggling to produce work they feel is good enough. They might be reading everything, watching every video, participating in discussions, but then freeze when it comes to actually submitting. Perfectionism combined with anxiety could definitely cause that pattern. Or they might be dealing with personal issues that drain their energy for completing assignments.
Q21: That actually resonates with my experience somewhat. I didn't have the highest LMS activity, but I performed well on exams and got a distinction. A lot of my learning happened offline with my notes and downloaded materials. Someone looking at my login frequency might think I wasn't engaged, but I was putting in serious work—just not always in ways the system could track.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're most active, whether you're meeting deadlines, and how much time you spend on videos. But they miss the quality of engagement—whether you're actually absorbing material or just going through the motions. They can't capture offline studying, emotional struggles, or the mental effort behind each login. They show behavior, not understanding or wellbeing.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what I'm learning and putting in genuine effort to understand it, not just complete it. It's when I'm curious about the material and thinking about it even outside of study time. For me, engagement also means pushing through on hard days even when my anxiety is telling me to give up. It's a mental and emotional investment, not just clicking through content.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled emotionally just to show up some days. It wouldn't capture the CBT sessions that helped me manage my anxiety enough to keep going. It can't see the hours I spent with printed notes, the tears of frustration, or the small victories when a concept finally clicked. My resilience and the internal battle I fought every week—none of that appears in any log.
